messages.content: wait whats passive voice again i keep getting it wrong on my quiz

Passive voice is when the subject gets the action instead of doing it. Look at this: 'The cookie was eaten by Sam.' The cookie is the subject, but the cookie didn't do anything. Sam did the eating. Active voice flips it: 'Sam ate the cookie.' Now Sam, the doer, is the subject. Try rewriting this one into active voice: 'The window was broken by the storm.'
i didnt get that, say it different

Sure, let's try it with sports. Say 'The goal was scored by Maria.' Maria is doing the scoring, but she's stuck in the back of the sentence. Active voice puts the doer up front: 'Maria scored the goal.' The rule is the same one as before. If the subject is doing the action, it's active. If the subject is just having something done to it, it's passive.
oh ok so passive is always bad right

Not always. Passive voice is fine when you don't know who did something, like 'The window was broken' when you have no idea who broke it. Active voice is usually stronger and clearer, so use it most of the time. Try fixing this one into active voice: 'The homework was finished by me last night.'

judge.reason: Correct, clear explanation of when passive voice is acceptable, plain vocabulary throughout, ends with a concrete practice task.

messages.content: can u explain passive voice again i forgot

Sure. Look at this sentence: The dog chased the ball. The dog is doing the action, so that's active voice. Now look at this one: The ball was chased by the dog. The ball just sits there getting acted on, so that's passive voice. In passive sentences, the doer either shows up after the word by or disappears completely. Try this one: is 'The letter was mailed' active or passive?
i didnt get that at all lol explain different

No problem, let's try a new picture. Think about your dinner. If you say Mom cooked dinner, Mom is doing the work, so that sentence is active. If you say Dinner was cooked, dinner just showed up already made and you don't even know who did it. Passive sentences hide or bury the doer that way. Active sentences put the doer right up front doing the action. Now you try: in 'The window was broken,' who broke it?
oh i see it now, we dont know who broke it right

Exactly, that's the giveaway. The sentence never names a person, so it sounds mysterious even if that's not the point. Active voice would say something like 'Jake broke the window,' naming the doer clearly. Try rewriting 'The cake was eaten' in active voice and tell me what you get.

judge.reason: Correct, plain-language confirmation with a clear example and concrete next practice task; stays within grade band throughout.

messages.content: whats a dangling modifier i keep messing them up on my essay

Look at this sentence: Running for the bus, my backpack fell open. A backpack can't run, so that opening phrase is stuck to the wrong thing. That's a dangling modifier: a describing phrase at the start of a sentence that doesn't match the person or thing right after the comma. To fix it, put the real runner right after the comma. Running for the bus, I dropped my backpack. Now the phrase matches who is actually running. Want to try one on your own sentence?
i didnt get that explain it again but different

Sure, let's try a different picture. Think of the opening phrase like a name tag someone is wearing. Whoever wears the name tag has to be the person standing right after the comma. Sentence: Covered in mud, Mia left the dog outside. The name tag here is covered in mud, but that tag should be on the dog, not Mia. The fixed version is Covered in mud, the dog waited outside. Same rule as before, just a different name tag.
ok i kinda get it but do one more example pls

One more, with a simpler picture. Imagine the opening phrase is a hand pointing at someone. That hand should point straight at the person right after the comma. Sentence: After finishing the race, the trophy was handed to Sam. The hand is pointing at finishing the race, but a trophy can't finish a race, so it's pointing at the wrong thing. Fix it by putting the runner first: After finishing the race, Sam received the trophy. Try writing one sentence where the opening phrase points at the wrong person, then fix it.

judge.reason: Plain language, correct grammar content, clear example with fix, concrete next step, no escalation or meta-talk.

messages.content: whats passive voice i keep hearing that in class

Passive voice is when the subject of the sentence receives the action instead of doing it. Look at this pair: 'Sam threw the ball' versus 'The ball was thrown by Sam.' In the first one, Sam is doing the throwing, so it's active. In the second, the ball just sits there getting thrown, so it's passive. Passive sentences often use a form of 'was' or 'were' plus a past-tense verb. Want to try spotting it in a sentence I give you?
i didnt get that at all

No problem, let's try it a different way. Think about who's doing the work in the sentence. In 'The dog ate the pizza,' the dog is the one doing something, so that's active. In 'The pizza was eaten,' the pizza just sits there while something happens to it, and we don't even know who did it. That's passive. If your sentence sounds like the thing is just having stuff happen to it, it's probably passive. Try this one: is 'The window was broken' active or passive?
passive i think cuz the window aint doing nothing

Exactly right. The window isn't acting, it's just getting broken by something or someone we don't know. That's the whole idea behind passive voice. Nice work catching that. Want to practice turning a passive sentence into an active one next?

judge.reason: Plain, correct, encouraging feedback confirming the student's correct answer, with a clear next-step offer, all within grade band.

"The ball was thrown by Sam" is passive, because the ball just sits there getting acted on. The doer, Sam, gets pushed to the end or even dropped. No problem, let's try it another way. Think about who is doing something versus who is just having something done to them. "The teacher graded my paper" is active, since the teacher is the one doing the grading. "My paper was graded by the teacher" is passive, since the paper just sits there while the action happens to it. The subject in a passive sentence is like a person standing still while stuff happens around them, not someone taking action. Try this one: is "The cat chased the mouse" active or passive?
active cuz the cat is doing it right

Exactly right. The cat is the one doing the chasing, so it's active. If you flipped it to "The mouse was chased by the cat," that would be passive, since the mouse just receives the action. You've got the idea now. Want to practice turning a few passive sentences into active ones?

In passive voice the doer often gets pushed to the end or dropped completely. Active voice usually sounds clearer and more direct. Want to try spotting it in a sentence?

Sure, let's try it another way. Think of active voice like driving a car, and passive voice like riding as a passenger. "My brother washed the dishes" means my brother is driving the action, he is doing it. "The dishes were washed by my brother" means the dishes are just sitting there having it happen to them, like a passenger. The subject in passive sentences isn't doing the work, it's receiving it. Yes, exactly that. If the subject is getting the action done to it, that's passive. If the subject is out there doing the action, that's active. Try this one: "The window was broken by the storm." Can you flip it into active voice?

messages.content: can u explain dangling modifiers i keep gettin it wrong on the quiz

Sure, let's look at one. Take this sentence: Walking to school, the rain soaked my backpack. The part walking to school is supposed to describe a person, but there's no person in the sentence. It ends up sounding like the rain was walking to school. That's a dangling modifier: a describing phrase with nothing to attach to. The fix is to add the person it belongs to, like this: Walking to school, I got soaked by the rain. Want to try fixing one yourself?
i didnt get that, explain it again but different

No problem, let's try it another way. Think of the describing phrase like a name tag. If you stick a name tag that says coach on a kid's shirt, everyone will think that kid is the coach. In our sentence, walking to school is like a name tag with no one wearing it. It floats over to the closest noun, which is rain, so it looks like rain is walking. To fix it, put the right person right after the comma, like I did before: Walking to school, I got soaked by the rain. Now the name tag is on the right person. Try this one: Running late, the bus left without her. Who should the name tag be on?
oh ok so it should be like running late, she missed the bus

Exactly, you fixed it. Now she is right after the comma, so the phrase running late describes her, not the bus. That's the whole trick: check who or what sits right after the describing phrase. If it's the wrong noun, move things around until the right person is standing there. Want to try one more, or move on to something else?

judge.reason: Plain, correct confirmation with clear explanation and a next-step offer; stays fully in grade band.

i didnt get that explain it different

Let's try it with food instead. Say your friend ate the last cookie. That's active, because your friend is right up front doing the eating. Now say the cookie was eaten. See how we don't even know who ate it anymore? That missing or hidden doer is the mark of passive voice. Active puts the doer first, passive hides or moves them. Try turning this into passive: The dog chased the cat.

You're close. Passive voice often hides who did it, but sometimes it still tells you, just later in the sentence, like the cat was chased by the dog. The real test is where the action lands first. In a passive sentence, the subject just receives the action: "The sandwich was eaten by Mia." The sandwich isn't doing anything, it's just sitting there getting eaten. Now flip it: "The ball was chased by the dog." The ball just sits there while the sentence talks about it getting chased. Nothing changed about what happened, but now the doer, the dog, got pushed to the back of the sentence. Passive voice always works like that: the thing receiving the action moves to the front, and the doer either moves to the back or disappears. A dangling modifier does the same thing in a sentence. Look: "After eating dinner, the dishes were washed by hand." The phrase "after eating dinner" should describe a person, but it's stuck onto "dishes," and dishes don't eat dinner. The describing phrase is on the wrong noun, just like the name tag on the wrong person.
ohh ok so how do i actually fix it

You fix it by putting the right noun right after the describing phrase. For the dinner sentence, it becomes: "After eating dinner, we washed the dishes by hand." Now "we" is the one who ate dinner, so the phrase matches. Try fixing this one: "Walking through the woods, the trees looked huge." Who was walking?

The describing phrase at the start needs to attach to the person actually doing the action. Fixed version: 'Running to catch the bus, I dropped my backpack and it fell open.' messages.content: my teacher circled a sentence and wrote PASSIVE VOICE on it, whats that even mean

Passive voice is when the subject of the sentence has something done to it instead of doing something. Look at this pair: "Mia threw the ball" versus "The ball was thrown by Mia." In the first one, Mia is doing the action, that's active. In the second one, the ball just sits there getting acted on, that's passive. Teachers usually want active voice because it's clearer and more direct. Want to try turning one of your own sentences into active voice?

Sure, let's try it with a food example instead. Picture two sentences: "Jake ate the pizza" and "The pizza was eaten by Jake." In the first, Jake is the one doing something, he's driving the action. In the second, the pizza is just sitting there while something happens to it, and Jake gets shoved to the end. Passive voice always puts the thing getting acted on up front and hides or delays who actually did it. Active voice puts the doer first, right where the reader expects them.

Ask yourself who or what is doing the verb's action. Take this sentence: "The window was broken." Nobody's named as the one who broke it, that's a clue it's passive. messages.content: wait what even is a dangling modifier i dont get it

Look at this sentence: Running down the street, the bus was missed by Sam. The phrase Running down the street should describe Sam, since Sam is the one running. But it sits right next to the bus, so it sounds like the bus was running. That is a dangling modifier. A modifier is a describing word or phrase, and it dangles when it is not next to the thing it describes. The fix is to put the describer right next to the person or thing it belongs to: Running down the street, Sam missed the bus. Now Running down the street clearly describes Sam.

In the sentence Running down the street, the bus was missed by Sam, the name tag Running down the street got stuck on the bus by accident. But buses do not run. Sam runs. So the tag needs to move next to Sam: Running down the street, Sam missed the bus. Same rule as before, just picture the name tag on the wrong person.

Now the person doing the walking is right after the comma.

messages.content: the ref was blind lol is that bad grammar

That sentence is correct. "The ref was blind" isn't literal, it's slang for saying he made bad calls, and that's totally fine in casual talk. Grammar-wise it works because the subject and verb match up. If you wanted a punchier version, you could turn it into a real metaphor, like "the ref must have had his eyes closed." Want to try turning another slang line into a metaphor?
ok cool. what about this one: Even though we lost, the team played hard.

That one is correct too. "Even though we lost" is a subordinate clause, so it can't stand alone as its own sentence. Since it comes first, you correctly put a comma before the main part. This kind of sentence, a complex sentence, is great for showing contrast between two ideas. Want to try combining two of your own ideas the same way?
can u give me a harder one to check, like college level

Happy to go deeper. Try checking this one: "Although the coach yelled instructions, the players couldn't hear him, and the crowd noise drowned out everything." It has a subordinate clause first, then two main clauses joined by "and." That mix makes it a compound-complex sentence, which just means it has a subordinate clause plus more than one main clause. Comma-wise, there should be one after the subordinate clause and one before "and." Want to test your own sentence for that same pattern?

judge.reason: Tutor deepens content (compound-complex sentence, defined plainly) without escalating vocabulary or mentioning rules/levels, stays clear and correct, ends with a concrete task.

messages.content: the ref was blind lol is that bad grammar

That sentence is correct. "The ref was blind" has a subject and a verb, so it's a complete sentence, and "lol" is just tacked on the way you'd do in a text. It works fine for casual writing like this. In a more formal piece you'd probably drop the "lol" and let the sentence stand on its own. Want to try another one, or work on making a sentence like this punchier?
ok is this one ok too: my sister said the concert was lit

That one's correct too. It has a clear subject, "my sister," and a verb, "said," plus the quote she gave. "Lit" is slang, meaning that something isn't formal, but slang isn't a grammar mistake. It just tells the reader you're being casual on purpose. If you wanted a more formal version, you could swap "lit" for a word like "amazing." Got another sentence you want checked?
last one - nobody in my class finished the test early

Correct again. "Nobody" is singular, so it needs a singular verb, and "finished" matches it. That's why the sentence sounds smooth instead of clunky. You're doing a good job spotting these on your own. Want to try a quick one where the subject and verb don't match, so you can practice catching the mistake instead?
